title: The Man Who Bends Light for a Living
summary: In a damp workshop in Warsaw, one of the last neon-tube benders explains why the craft is dying not because the skill is hard, but because almost nobody now wants what it makes.
body:
Tomasz Wieczorek has been bending glass tubing over an open flame for thirty-one years, and he is unsentimental about the fact that the craft will probably die with him. His workshop, in a courtyard behind a tram depot in the Praga district of Warsaw, smells of scorched dust and the faint sweetness of the gas he feeds into the finished tubes. It is not, he insists, a difficult skill to acquire. A patient apprentice can learn the basic bends in a year. What cannot be taught, he says, is the market — and the market for hand-bent neon collapsed quietly, without anyone declaring it dead.

What killed the trade was not, as people assume, the light-emitting diode. LED strips are cheaper and brighter, but Tomasz argues that they were merely the instrument, not the cause. The cause was that the kind of business that once commissioned a neon sign — the corner pharmacy, the family hotel, the cinema with one screen — stopped existing in the form that needed one. A chain does not order a custom sign; it ships an identical illuminated panel to four hundred locations. Neon was always the signature of the particular, and it is the particular, not the technology, that has been retreating.

He is careful to resist the romance that visitors bring with them. Film crews and design students arrive expecting a melancholy craftsman lamenting a lost golden age, and he disappoints them on purpose. Much of the old neon, he points out, was ugly, badly made, and maintained by nobody. The glow that nostalgia now treats as warm and human was, at the time, simply what a cheap advert looked like. It is only because the alternative became so relentlessly uniform that the old imperfection began to seem like character.

There is, however, one thing he will concede to the romantics. A hand-bent tube carries a record of the hand that bent it. No two of his letters are quintessentially identical; a practised eye can tell his work from that of his late rival across the river. A printed panel carries no such trace, and this, he thinks, is the real loss — not the warmth of the light, which can be imitated, but the evidence of a decision having been made by a person. When the last of the old signs is finally taken down, what will vanish is not a style but a kind of authorship that nobody thought to value while it was ordinary.

He still takes the occasional order: a museum reconstruction, a wealthy collector's private bar, a film set that wants the real thing rather than a digital fake. These commissions pay well and mean little to him. The work he would choose, if choosing were possible, is the work that no longer exists — the unglamorous job of lettering a name above a doorway for someone who would walk past it every day and never once look up. That, he says, is the difference between a craft that is alive and a craft that is merely preserved.

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences for emphasis ('What killed the trade was…', 'it is the particular that…')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ (cleft sentence) dùng 'What … was …' hoặc 'It is … that …' để nhấn mạnh một thành phần trong câu. Bài viết dùng nhiều lần: 'What killed the trade was not … the light-emitting diode' và 'it is the particular, not the technology, that has been retreating'. Cấu trúc này cho phép tác giả dời trọng tâm sang yếu tố muốn nhấn, một thủ pháp B2 điển hình của văn nghị luận.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Cleft sentences use 'What … was …' or 'It is … that …' to spotlight one element of a clause. The passage relies on them repeatedly: 'What killed the trade was not, as people assume, the light-emitting diode' and 'it is the particular, not the technology, that has been retreating.' The structure shifts emphasis onto the chosen element — a hallmark of B2 argumentative writing.

translationVi:
Tomasz Wieczorek đã uốn cong ống thủy tinh trên ngọn lửa trần suốt ba mươi mốt năm, và ông không hề bi lụy về việc nghề này có lẽ sẽ chết cùng ông. Xưởng của ông, nằm trong một cái sân sau một bến tàu điện ở quận Praga của Warsaw, sực mùi bụi cháy xém và mùi ngòn ngọt thoảng của thứ khí ông bơm vào những ống đã hoàn thành. Ông quả quyết rằng đây không phải một kỹ năng khó học. Một người học việc kiên nhẫn có thể học các đường uốn cơ bản trong một năm. Thứ không thể dạy được, ông nói, là thị trường — và thị trường cho đèn neon uốn tay đã sụp đổ một cách lặng lẽ, không ai tuyên bố nó đã chết.

Thứ giết chết cái nghề này không phải, như người ta vẫn nghĩ, là đi-ốt phát quang. Dải LED rẻ hơn và sáng hơn, nhưng Tomasz lập luận rằng chúng chỉ là công cụ, không phải nguyên nhân. Nguyên nhân là loại hình kinh doanh từng đặt một biển neon — hiệu thuốc đầu phố, khách sạn gia đình, rạp chiếu một màn hình — đã ngừng tồn tại ở dạng cần đến nó. Một chuỗi cửa hàng không đặt một biển riêng; nó vận chuyển một tấm bảng chiếu sáng giống hệt nhau đến bốn trăm địa điểm. Neon luôn là dấu ấn của cái riêng biệt, và chính cái riêng biệt, chứ không phải công nghệ, mới là thứ đang rút lui.

Ông cẩn thận cưỡng lại sự lãng mạn hóa mà khách tham quan mang theo. Các đoàn làm phim và sinh viên thiết kế đến với kỳ vọng gặp một người thợ u sầu than tiếc một thời hoàng kim đã mất, và ông cố ý làm họ thất vọng. Phần lớn đèn neon cũ, ông chỉ ra, là xấu, làm ẩu và chẳng ai bảo dưỡng. Cái ánh sáng mà sự hoài niệm bây giờ coi là ấm áp và đầy tính người thì khi đó chỉ đơn giản là vẻ ngoài của một quảng cáo rẻ tiền. Chỉ vì cái thay thế nó trở nên đồng nhất một cách không khoan nhượng mà sự khiếm khuyết cũ kỹ kia mới bắt đầu có vẻ giống một nét cá tính.

Tuy vậy, có một điều ông sẽ nhượng bộ những người lãng mạn. Một ống uốn tay mang một dấu vết của bàn tay đã uốn nó. Không có hai chữ cái nào của ông giống hệt nhau; một con mắt lành nghề có thể phân biệt tác phẩm của ông với của người đối thủ quá cố bên kia sông. Một tấm bảng in không mang dấu vết nào như vậy, và đây, ông nghĩ, mới là mất mát thực sự — không phải sự ấm áp của ánh sáng, thứ có thể bắt chước, mà là bằng chứng rằng một quyết định đã được một con người đưa ra. Khi tấm biển cũ cuối cùng cuối cùng bị tháo xuống, thứ biến mất sẽ không phải một phong cách mà là một dạng quyền tác giả mà chẳng ai nghĩ đến việc trân trọng khi nó còn bình thường.

Ông vẫn nhận đơn hàng thi thoảng: một phục dựng bảo tàng, một quầy bar riêng của nhà sưu tập giàu có, một phim trường muốn đồ thật thay vì đồ giả kỹ thuật số. Những đơn này trả tiền hậu hĩnh và chẳng có ý nghĩa mấy với ông. Công việc ông sẽ chọn, nếu được chọn, là công việc không còn tồn tại nữa — cái việc không hào nhoáng là viết một cái tên trên một khung cửa cho một người sẽ đi ngang qua nó mỗi ngày và không một lần ngước nhìn lên. Đó, ông nói, là khác biệt giữa một nghề còn sống và một nghề chỉ đơn thuần được bảo tồn.

questions.prompt: How does Tomasz feel about the likely death of his craft?
options: Unsentimental — he accepts it without lament
Optimistic that it will be revived
Indifferent because he never enjoyed it
questions.explanation: 'he is unsentimental about the fact that the craft will probably die with him.'

questions.prompt: According to Tomasz, what is the hard part of the trade?
questions.explanation: 'What cannot be taught, he says, is the market.'

questions.prompt: What does Tomasz say actually killed the trade?
options: The LED, which he blames directly
The disappearance of the kind of particular business that once commissioned signs
questions.explanation: 'the kind of business that once commissioned a neon sign … stopped existing in the form that needed one.'

questions.prompt: What role does Tomasz assign to the LED?
options: Merely the instrument, not the cause
questions.explanation: 'they were merely the instrument, not the cause.'

questions.prompt: How does a chain business differ from the old corner shop, per the passage?
options: It commissions more elaborate custom signs
It ships an identical illuminated panel to all locations
It refuses to use illuminated signage
questions.explanation: 'it ships an identical illuminated panel to four hundred locations.'

questions.prompt: Why does Tomasz deliberately disappoint visiting film crews and students?
options: He refuses to perform the melancholy-craftsman role they expect
He is too busy to talk
questions.explanation: 'they arrive expecting a melancholy craftsman … and he disappoints them on purpose.'

questions.prompt: What does Tomasz say much of the old neon actually was?
options: Ugly, badly made, and maintained by nobody
questions.explanation: 'Much of the old neon, he points out, was ugly, badly made, and maintained by nobody.'

questions.prompt: Why does the old imperfection now 'seem like character'?
options: Because the alternative became relentlessly uniform
questions.explanation: 'It is only because the alternative became so relentlessly uniform that the old imperfection began to seem like character.'

questions.prompt: What single point does Tomasz concede to the romantics?
options: That the light itself is irreplaceably warm
That a hand-bent tube carries a record of the hand that bent it
That neon is better than LED
questions.explanation: 'A hand-bent tube carries a record of the hand that bent it.'

questions.prompt: According to Tomasz, what is the real loss when neon disappears?
options: The warmth of the light, which cannot be imitated
The evidence of a decision having been made by a person — a kind of authorship
questions.explanation: 'not the warmth of the light, which can be imitated, but the evidence of a decision having been made by a person.'

questions.prompt: What kind of commissions does Tomasz still accept?
options: Museum reconstructions, collectors' bars, and film sets
questions.explanation: 'a museum reconstruction, a wealthy collector's private bar, a film set.'

questions.prompt: Which work would Tomasz choose if he could?
options: The unglamorous job of lettering a name above an everyday doorway
questions.explanation: 'the unglamorous job of lettering a name above a doorway for someone who would walk past it every day.'

questions.prompt: What distinction does the final line draw?
options: Between a craft that is alive and one that is merely preserved
questions.explanation: 'the difference between a craft that is alive and a craft that is merely preserved.'

questions.prompt: The phrase 'the signature of the particular' (paragraph 2) is closest in meaning to:
options: The distinctive mark of individual, one-off things
questions.explanation: Neon marked the individual, non-standardised business — 'the particular' as opposed to the chain.
questions.hint: Read it against 'a chain does not order a custom sign'.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the passage NOT claiming?
options: That the LED was the true cause of neon's decline
That nostalgia reinterprets old cheapness as character
That hand-bent work carries traces of its maker
That standardisation, not technology, drove the loss
questions.explanation: Tomasz explicitly says the LED was 'merely the instrument, not the cause' — the passage denies that the LED was the true cause.
questions.hint: Compare with the second paragraph's argument about cause versus instrument.

questions.prompt: What is the overall tone of the piece?
options: Clear-eyed and resistant to easy nostalgia, while granting one genuine loss
questions.explanation: Tomasz resists romance ('disappoints them on purpose') yet concedes the loss of authorship.
questions.hint: Note how he both deflates and concedes.

cloze.template:
Tomasz Wieczorek has been bending glass tubing over an open flame for thirty-one years, and he is {{b1}} about the fact that the craft will probably die with him. His workshop, in a courtyard behind a tram depot in the Praga district of Warsaw, smells of scorched dust. It is not, he insists, a difficult skill to {{b2}}. A patient apprentice can learn the basic bends in a year. What cannot be taught, he says, is the {{b3}} — and the market for hand-bent neon collapsed quietly, without anyone declaring it dead.

What killed the trade was not, as people assume, the light-emitting diode. LED strips are cheaper and brighter, but Tomasz argues that they were merely the {{b4}}, not the cause. The cause was that the kind of business that once {{b5}} a neon sign stopped existing in the form that needed one. A chain does not order a custom sign; it ships an {{b6}} illuminated panel to four hundred locations. Neon was always the signature of the {{b7}}, and it is the particular, not the technology, that has been {{b8}}.

He is careful to resist the romance that visitors bring. Film crews and design students arrive expecting a melancholy craftsman {{b9}} a lost golden age, and he disappoints them on purpose. Much of the old neon, he points out, was ugly, badly made, and maintained by nobody. It is only because the alternative became so relentlessly {{b10}} that the old imperfection began to seem like character.

There is one thing he will {{b11}} to the romantics. A hand-bent tube carries a record of the hand that bent it. A practised {{b12}} can tell his work from that of his late rival. A printed panel carries no such {{b13}}, and this is the real loss — not the warmth of the light, which can be imitated, but the evidence of a decision having been made by a person, a kind of {{b14}} that nobody thought to value while it was ordinary.

The work he would choose, if choosing were possible, is the work that no longer exists — the {{b15}} job of lettering a name above a doorway for someone who would walk past it every day and never once look up.

blanks.explanation: 'a melancholy craftsman lamenting a lost golden age.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Tomasz argues that the real loss is not the warm light but the visible 'evidence of a decision having been made by a person' — a kind of authorship we only value once it is gone. Where in your own surroundings — fonts, buildings, food, music — has the trace of an individual maker been replaced by something standardised? Did you notice the change while it was happening, or only afterwards?
